findings.description: login() builds its SQL via an f-string interpolating the raw email, a textbook SQL injection (e.g. email "' OR '1'='1' --") right next to the hmac/pbkdf2 security theater.
validate_session inverts the expiry check: a still-valid session (expires_at in the future) is deleted and rejected while an expired one is accepted, causing auth bypass plus single-use sessions.
LOCKOUT_MINUTES is declared but never used and no lock timestamp is stored, so a locked account stays locked forever with no time-based reset path.
find_user_by_email runs SELECT * FROM users, materializes the entire user table in memory, then linear-scans in Python instead of using WHERE email = ?, an O(n) full-table load per lookup.
PBKDF2 hashing runs only after a user row is found, so valid emails cost ~100k iterations and invalid ones return instantly, creating a timing oracle for user enumeration.
Differential logging/early-return between the unknown-email path and the wrong-password path leaks account existence via behavior and timing.
Sessions live only in an in-memory dict, so they vanish on restart, do not share across workers/processes, and cleanup_expired must be invoked manually by something that never calls it.
datetime.utcnow() is deprecated in 3.12+ and returns naive datetimes, which will raise once compared against any tz-aware datetime introduced downstream.
Inconsistent typing style: typing.Optional is imported and used while built-in generics (dict[str, Session]) are used natively elsewhere.
